cover.title: THE SUN
cover.subtitle: The one everyone calls good, and no one thinks to check on.
cover.tagline: You make everyone comfortable. You're still learning how to let someone do that for you too.

profile_scores.caption: High care, low expression and pride. You reach out and take care of others first, while your own needs often come last.

short_summary: You make people comfortable without even trying, and the closer someone gets, the more you wonder how much they actually know about you.
You're not exhausted because you cared too much. You're exhausted because you were always the one moving first.
What's missing isn't more warmth. It's asking yourself the same question you keep asking everyone else: how am I doing right now?

full_report.headline: THE SUN
full_report.intro: You're someone who makes people comfortable. Most people never notice how much you do to make that happen. This report follows what that can cost you: why the people you love can know so much about you while your own needs keep getting pushed back, and what starts to change when care finally moves both ways.

chapters.title: When You Love
body: You're someone who makes people comfortable. In an awkward room, you're the one who speaks first. When the mood dips, you slip in a joke. When someone feels left out, you naturally make room for them. Here's the strange part: people barely notice you doing it. It comes so naturally that it disappears into the background.

You're the same way when you love someone. You don't make them feel like a burden. You notice what they need before they ask. When they look down, you act before you even ask why. So a lot of people remember you as a good person.
But the closer you get to someone, a certain thought starts to creep in. "I know so much about them. How much do they actually know about me?"
chapters.profile_note: High care, low expression and pride. You reach out and take care of others first, while your own needs often come last.

chapters.title: The Pattern You Keep Repeating
body: You're easy to love at the start of a relationship. Being together is comfortable. Conversation flows easily. The mood is good. The problem starts after that.
When they're struggling, you're the first to notice. When they're worn out, you're the first to act. When they're upset, you're the first to smooth it over.
Then, at some point, something strange happens. The relationship keeps deepening, but you keep becoming less visible inside it.
Their feelings come first. Yours keep getting pushed back.
And one day you find yourself wondering, "When was the last time anyone took care of me?"
chapters.highlight: You're not exhausted because there wasn't enough love. You're exhausted because you were always the one moving first.

chart.title: THE INVISIBLE CARE CURVE
chart.description: As the relationship deepens, your attention toward them keeps growing, while your own state gets pushed further back.

chapters.title: What You've Gotten Wrong About Yourself

items.belief: I'm just naturally more comfortable giving than receiving.
items.truth: Giving really can feel natural to you. But receiving often feels more awkward than you admit. Think about it. Hasn't there been a time when someone tried to take care of you, and you quickly said, "I'm fine"? You weren't trying to reject them. You just weren't used to letting someone step in.
items.truth_short: Giving feels natural. Receiving still takes practice.

items.belief: If I just understand them a little more, it'll be fine.
items.truth: Understanding matters. But one person can't do all of it. If you're always the one explaining, forgiving, and adjusting, the relationship may become easier to keep going without becoming healthier.
items.truth_short: Understanding helps when both people are doing it.

items.belief: If I say I'm struggling, it'll be a burden.
items.truth: You've probably told yourself that more than once. But someone who cares about you needs a real chance to know what you're carrying. They may not respond perfectly the first time. What matters is whether they listen, come back, and make room for you too.
items.truth_short: Sharing what you're carrying gives the relationship a chance to become more mutual.

chapters.title: The Person You Keep Choosing
body: You're especially drawn to people who don't express much. People who care, but rarely say it. People who have feelings, but expect you to notice them first.
At first, it feels special. You notice things other people miss. A change in their voice. A longer pause. A different expression. You fill in the blanks.
But the more you do that, the less they have to say.
And the more responsibility you take on without anyone asking whether you want it.
The real question isn't whether they appreciate your warmth once. It's whether they slowly learn how to notice and care for you too.
chapters.highlight: Someone who likes your warmth receives it. Someone who loves you learns how to give warmth back.

chapters.title: What Actually Changes Something
body: What you need isn't to get even warmer. You already have that covered.
What you need is to check in with yourself too.
"How am I doing right now?"
Ask yourself that as often as you ask about their mood or their day.
Are you tired today? Is something bothering you? Are you holding something in?
Then say one small part of it out loud.
This isn't selfish. It's how you keep yourself present inside the relationship.
chapters.action_sentence: This isn't selfish. It's how you keep yourself present inside the relationship.

chapters.title: Where You Go From Here
body: You already know how to give love. You already know how to make someone comfortable. You already know how to make a relationship feel warm.
What you're learning next is different.
How to receive love without brushing it away with "I'm fine."
How to let someone help before you reach the point of exhaustion.
How to say what you need while it's still small enough to share.
You don't have to become less strong. You're learning how to let strength and care move both ways.
chapters.transition_note: You've spent a long time taking care of other people. Now you're learning how to let someone take care of you too.

chapters.title: The Types You Keep Falling For

items.type_title: The Person Who Rarely Expresses Anything
items.description: They don't say much. But once in a while, they show a tiny sign that means everything to you. At first, it feels romantic. Eventually, you're doing all the interpreting.

items.type_title: The Person Who's Going Through A Hard Time
items.description: They're exhausted. Something happened. They need support. And before you realize it, you're taking care of them. One day, the relationship feels more like healing work than love.

items.type_title: The Person Who's Used to Receiving
items.description: They're not always cruel. Sometimes they're simply used to being taken care of. But being used to something isn't the same as valuing it. And you can end up carrying that disappointment alone.

Everyone likes them. People always speak well of them. So when something hurts you, you start wondering if you're the difficult one. Their reputation doesn't erase what keeps happening between the two of you.

chapters.title: The Warning Signs You've Missed
chapters.intro_note: These signs showed up faster than you'd think. You just explained them away before they had a chance to register.

signal_strength.label: You know their day in detail; they barely know yours
You're reaching out first far more often than they are
You don't say it when something's hard, afraid it'll bring the mood down
You stay tuned in to their mood while putting your own on hold

chapters.core_signal: Your attention keeps moving toward them, while your own needs stay mostly out of sight.

chapters.title: What a Healthy Relationship Looks Like for You

checklist.title: They ask, and you answer.
checklist.description: They notice when something feels off, and you don't have to hide behind "I'm fine."

checklist.title: You can be the one who needs support.
checklist.description: You don't have to be cheerful or steady all the time. There's room for you to have a hard day too.

checklist.title: Care moves both ways.
checklist.description: You're not always the one noticing first, reaching out first, or fixing the mood first.

checklist.title: They remember what you shared.
checklist.description: They don't just listen once. They come back to it, follow through, and make changes you can actually feel.

chapters.title: What Happens In Your Next Relationship

sections.who_breaks_you: You're vulnerable to someone who's slow to respond, slow to express gratitude, and happy to let you fill the empty space. You can keep that kind of relationship moving for a long time. But eventually, it wears you down.
sections.who_steadies_you: A better match is someone who takes care of you back. They reach out first sometimes. They check in without waiting for a crisis. They notice the small things and actually do something with what they learn.
sections.same_pattern_repeats: You may still feel drawn to people who need support or don't express much. That doesn't decide how the relationship will end. What matters is whether you stay visible after the connection begins, and whether they learn how to care for you too.
sections.next_signs: This time, the signs may show up sooner. You keep reaching out first, setting the mood, and making the plans. They keep receiving it. One moment doesn't tell you much. The pattern does.
sections.try_this: Pick one real need and say it plainly. Ask for a check in, practical help, or time to talk. Don't turn it into a test. Then watch what happens over time. Do they listen? Do they remember? Do they start showing up without being told every time?
sections.final_line: The answer isn't one reaction. It's whether care starts moving both ways.

action_plan.summary: You already know how to take care of someone. What's left is letting yourself be cared for too.
action_items: Ask yourself, "How am I doing right now?" at least once today.
Next time someone offers to help, don't rush to say "I'm fine." Let them help with one thing.
Say one real need out loud and watch whether care becomes more mutual over time.

closing.message: You've spent a long time learning how to take care of other people. This report looked at why your own needs kept getting pushed back, and how that pattern starts to change when you let yourself be cared for too.
closing.cross_sell_cta: Curious what your partner's pattern looks like? See their Cosmic Blueprint.
